article_title: BrainGate Implant Lets Two Paralyzed People Type at 22 Words per Minute Using Attempted Finger Movements

content_preview.title: BrainGate Implant Lets Two Paralyzed People Type at 22 Words per Minute Using Attempted Finger Movements
content_preview.summary: A brain-computer interface developed by Mass General Brigham and Brown University decoded intended finger movements into QWERTY keystrokes at 110 characters per minute with a 1.6 percent word error rate, offering a faster alternative to eye-gaze systems for people with ALS and spinal cord injuries.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Researchers from Mass General Brigham and Brown University have demonstrated an implantable brain-computer interface that enabled two people with paralysis to type on a virtual QWERTY keyboard by attempting finger movements, achieving speeds of up to 110 characters per minute — or roughly 22 words per minute — with a word error rate of just 1.6 percent. The results, [published in Nature Neuroscience](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41593-026-02218-y) on March 16, 2026, represent a ...

editor_notes.source_verification: Brown University (fetched): Confirms 22 WPM, 110 chars/min, 1.6% WER, QWERTY, two participants (ALS + SCI), Nature Neuroscience March 16, Mass General Brigham, attempted finger movements, 30-sentence calibration. STAT News (partial paywall): Confirms BCI trends, commercial landscape. Nature Neuroscience: academic source, not directly fetchable but confirmed by Brown press release.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All performance metrics verified against Brown University press release.
editor_notes.originality: No prior BCI/BrainGate coverage. Unique topic.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Strong neuroscience news. Ready for publication.
